intro:
Salvation is God’s deliverance of his people from danger, evil, and destruction — supremely from sin and its consequences. The Hebrew yāshaʻ ("to save, deliver") gives the name Yēshûaʻ / Jesus, and the Greek sōtēria carries the sense of rescue, healing, and wholeness. Throughout Scripture, "salvation belongs to the LORD" (Psalms 3:8) — it is his work from first to last.

In the Old Testament God reveals himself as Savior: the Exodus is the great paradigm — "stand firm, and see the salvation of the LORD" (Exodus 14:13) — and he rescues his people again and again. The prophets point beyond physical rescue to a deeper deliverance from sin, accomplished by the Suffering Servant (Isaiah 53:5) and sealed in a new covenant (Jeremiah 31:31–34).

In the New Testament salvation is the heart of the gospel and is found in Christ alone. His name means "the LORD saves," "for he will save his people from their sins" (Matthew 1:21); he came "to seek and to save the lost" (Luke 19:10); and "there is salvation in no one else" (Acts 4:12). It is "by grace… through faith… not a result of works" (Ephesians 2:8–9), accomplished through Christ’s blood (Romans 5:9) and received by all who call on him (Romans 10:9–13).

Scripture speaks of salvation in three tenses: believers have been saved from sin’s penalty — justified (Titus 3:5); they are being saved from sin’s power — sanctified (1 Corinthians 1:18; Philippians 2:12–13); and they will be saved from sin’s presence and the coming wrath — glorified (Romans 5:9; Hebrews 9:28). Planned by the Father, accomplished by the Son, and applied by the Spirit, salvation is the comprehensive saving work of the triune God, so that the redeemed cry, "Salvation belongs to our God" (Revelation 7:10).
